chapters.title: Food choices shape your body and mind
chapters.text: In 2018, nutritionists were puzzled. Research showed that almost half of Thai Buddhist monks were overweight and 10 percent had diabetes. How could that be? They stuck to tradition and fasted daily, so their meals had far fewer calories than the average Thai citizen. After watching what they actually ate, scientists found the cause. The monks indulged in soft drinks. We often focus on the number of calories, but the quality of those calories matters just as much. The 100 calories from sugar and fat affect the body differently, since sugar slows metabolism while fat does the opposite. As Dr. Mark Hyman has written, calories are information, instructions that affect hormones, brain chemistry, the immune system, the microbiome, gene expression, and metabolism. Despite eating a lot, more than 90 percent of Americans lack essential vitamins. What we have in excess are unhealthy additives, with each person consuming 3 to 5 pounds a year. As a result, we are more prone to chronic disease. Not all food harms us. Scientists in Philadelphia found that after serving patients nutritious meals for a year, hospital visits dropped by 50 percent, and when patients did come in, they spent 30 percent less time in the medical center. Carefully chosen food can be medicine. Healthy eating sounds simple in theory but is hard to put into practice. Food quality comes from economic, political, ecological, and social factors, and we have to tackle all of them if we want to live longer. Are you ready to discover the hidden truth about what you eat? A warning, it may shock you.

chapters.title: Unhealthy products are symptoms, the system is the cause
chapters.text: Have you ever wondered about the life story of a hamburger on your plate? It might be eye-opening. Until 1990, countless small seed companies existed, and their products added to species diversity and resilience. Over time, more than 100 of them ended up in the hands of four large corporations. Today, these corporations supply over 60 percent of seeds to farmers and sell the pesticides and herbicides used to protect the crops. Glyphosate is among the most popular, with Americans using more than 3.5 billion pounds of it on over 70 crops each year. That means you can find glyphosate in nearly everything, from bread to a sushi roll. Studies have shown that even 0.1 parts per billion can harm the microbiome. Less than 5 percent of American farmland grows fruits and vegetables. As the soil wears out, farmers add more chemicals, which may also come with the water used for irrigation. Crops soak up these toxic ingredients, so the bun on your hamburger is far from a healthy choice. The same pattern plays out on animal farms. Overcrowded and unsanitary conditions trigger disease, so workers add antibiotics to feed to keep cattle alive and growing. As a result, the meat carries drugs and chemicals. But that is not the end of the journey. Once it reaches kitchens, chefs add more additives and flavor enhancers before it lands on your plate at the cafe. Not every hamburger starts this way, but it is typical of many dishes. Why are we unaware? Some companies work hard to keep us in the dark. This story is a telling example that our food system is broken. Today it looks like a vicious cycle, with farmers and consumers trapped inside. Without government support, farmers often have no choice but to use pesticides, or they face bankruptcy. More herbicides mean worse soil, which means more chemicals the next season. Consumers unintentionally keep this model alive, since many people cannot afford organic produce. They pick the cheaper option and pay for it with their health. The first step in fighting this unfair system is seeing the link between politics, agriculture, and the environment, because food touches all three.

chapters.title: Practical ways to limit chemicals in your diet
chapters.text: Ultra-processed food is gaining popularity from Mexico to India, slowly replacing traditional diets, and in the US it now makes up almost two-thirds of our calories. The consequences are dramatic. In 1962, 3.4 percent of Americans were obese, and today that number is 40 percent. About half of Americans have pre-diabetes or type 2 diabetes, and even teenagers are affected, numbers that would have seemed unthinkable 30 years ago. The trend is global, and the situation is worse in some countries. In the 1990s, 1 in 150 Chinese had diabetes, and by 2030, that figure is expected to reach 1 in 10. As Dr. Mark Hyman has noted, your zip code is a bigger determinant of your health outcomes than your genetic code. So it is time to take control of what we eat. Follow these recommendations to improve your well-being. Eat up to fifteen servings of whole plants each day, choosing low-glycemic fruits like apples, cherries, grapefruits, and blackberries, and add whole grains to your plate. Make healthy fats a habit, starting with nuts and seeds, which lower disease risk, and add meat only if you can find a free-range farm that avoids chemicals. Eggs are also a good choice since they are packed with nutrients. Pick fish low in mercury, like herring and sardines, which are full of omega-3. Do not overlook the mineral and protein content of beans. Cut back on sugar in food and drinks, including juices, since studies show that one soda a day raises the risk of diabetes by more than 30 percent. Be careful with cow dairy and try goat or sheep options instead. Visit the Non-GMO Project website, an initiative that checks products and lists sellers who avoid GMOs, with a database of over 14,200 retailers. Read food labels and skip products with words you cannot understand, such as potassium bromate (E924), azodicarbonamide (ADA), BHA, BHT, brominated vegetable oil (BVO), yellow food dyes number 5 and 6, and red dye number 40, all of which carry serious risks. Limit plastic containers, since their chemicals can leach into food. Support small local farmers and people who push for organic food, using the LocalHarvest database to find them.

chapters.title: Why school lunches need an upgrade
chapters.text: Obesity rates have shot up over the last 50 years, and today, one in three children struggles with weight. A sedentary lifestyle is not the only reason. Look at school meals and ask why they are full of junk food. First, nutrition standards for school lunches are full of loopholes. They count ketchup, tomato paste, and french fries as vegetables. A Smart Snacks policy allows companies to sell junk food as long as the label says whole grain or reduced fat. Some schools have even negotiated with Domino Pizza about sales. Second, massive advertising is a culprit. Ads are everywhere, from TV to buses to schools, but the most dangerous space is digital. Marketers place ads in websites, movies, and videos. For example, Pokémon GO sent its players to McDonald because the company sponsored the game. Shockingly, this restaurant chain and Coca-Cola target even two-year-old children in their ads. In 2016, 56 companies paid for 3.4 billion promotional posts on social media, and they do it to build lifelong customers. A 30-second ad is enough for kids to choose that brand later, and if the ad repeats, they become devoted fans. Some cases show how we can tackle this challenge. Many Boston public schools had no kitchens, only microwaves, so they bought meals in plastic containers and heated them up. Businesswoman Jill Shah decided to change that and built the Hub and Spoke model, where schools with cooking equipment prepare food for the ones that lack it, which made meals healthier and created jobs. The Wellness in the Schools initiative is changing menus for pupils. In New York, culinary graduates work in school kitchens for three years, setting new cooking standards and teaching others. Another public-private partnership, Salad Bars to Schools, supplies schools with fresh vegetables. In Sweden, toy and junk food ads can target children only over 12, and any ad aimed at younger kids is illegal. There is more we can do in schools. Remove junk food and replace chocolate milk with white milk to cut sugar. Search for local organic farms. Organize a garden nearby. Add cooking skills and basic nutrition knowledge to the school program.

chapters.title: Healthy food should be within everyone reach
chapters.text: In 2018, researchers spotted a telling pattern. In poor districts near New York, ads for unhealthy drinks appear two to four times more often at the start of the month than at the end. In wealthier neighborhoods, the amount stays the same all month. Why do low-income families face more ads in the first week? Because food stamps go out then. Low-income individuals spend 75 percent of their income on ultra-processed food. Ads are just the surface. The real problem is price. When a bottle of water costs more than a can of sugar, we have a crisis. These problems hit hardest for the 140 million Americans who live near poverty. Another trap is distribution. People in some districts, known as food deserts, must drive miles to find vegetables, while ultra-processed food sits around the corner. This is a daily challenge for 23 million Americans. What should we demand from government to fix this? Introduce taxes on sugary drinks. Subsidize farmers so vegetables and fruits become cheaper. Ban the use of food stamps to buy sugary drinks and other junk products. Create incentives to eat well. In Massachusetts, low-income residents who buy fruits and vegetables get a financial bonus. A similar program, Double Up Food Bucks, runs in Michigan, where every $10 from food stamps becomes $20 if the shopper sticks to nutritious, locally grown food. Both consumers and small farmers win, and half the states have already launched similar programs. Install more public water stations. San Francisco has done this, adding 100 water stations across the city. Support and promote initiatives from food desert residents. For example, Ron Finley turns lawns into gardens. The West Oakland community launched People Grocery, a project of mobile stores that deliver vegetables and fruits, inspired by ice cream trucks but stocked with healthy products.

chapters.title: Corporate lobbyists put public health at risk
chapters.text: Many governments are trying to regulate junk food advertising and consumption. In Chile, junk food ads only run on TV at night. Nearly 30 countries tax soft drinks. Many European authorities have introduced simple food labels, green for healthy, yellow for neutral, and red for unhealthy. The American government has tried to follow suit but faces a giant obstacle, the lobbyists of large corporations, who spend enormous sums to influence policy. The cost of loopholes in the Farm Bill is $500 million. Lavish gifts and political campaign funding pave the way for lawmakers loyalty. For instance, Monsanto and Syngenta sponsored three-quarters of the Senate Committee members in charge of nutrition, and after Pepsi donated $5 million, Save the Children backed off the soda taxes campaign. Many lobbyists move freely between industry and government. Maggie Lyons, for example, first pushed the National Grocers Association agenda and later became an adviser to the US Department of Agriculture. The results of lobbying are easy to see. Toxic bisphenol-A, found in kids food, is still legal. Other chemicals are declared safe without testing. There are no labels for GMOs. Governments must change this. The following steps will reduce the damage. Ban officials from lobbying for at least five years after leaving office. Stop former lobbyists from working in government, even as advisers. Make anonymous funding impossible. Tax the sums spent on lobbying. Corporate power is not limited to politics. Although most scientific studies are reliable, it is wise to take their findings with a grain of salt, since companies fund articles, research centers, and universities. For example, the Pennington Biomedical Research Center claims that lack of exercise is the only cause of obesity, and it is no coincidence that the center received an almost $7 million grant from Coca-Cola. Large companies also set up new centers to push ideas that help their sales. Before you trust a claim that sugar is harmless, check who funded the authors or centers. As fake studies mislead communities, the scientific community needs to take the following steps. Scientists should share raw data so others can check the conclusions. Tighten conflict-of-interest rules. Encourage rigorous large trials instead of uncertain observational research.

chapters.title: Healthy soil grows healthy food and a healthier planet
chapters.text: Changing how we use natural resources is no longer optional. 33 percent of topsoil has already vanished in the last 150 years, and studies show the US has only about sixty harvests left before the soil can no longer grow food. On top of that, tilling and pesticides lead to chemical-laden harvests, a stronger greenhouse effect, and weaker soil that absorbs less water. The good news is that we can change it. A 2019 UN report shows that regenerative agriculture can restore the soil, and regenerated soil may contain 3 to 8 percent more organic matter. Each extra percent per acre can hold up to 27,000 gallons of water. The Main Street Project has already adopted regenerative agriculture and shares the experience with other farmers. The idea takes the best of traditional farming and improves it, using no-till methods, rotating crops to keep soil covered, and using grass-fed cattle, since grazing makes roots pump carbon and boosts soil quality. Another positive case is the nonprofit Kiss the Ground, which gives farmers the knowledge and support to use regenerative agriculture. Costa Rica has launched payments for people who help nature through reforestation and water management. The world needs to produce food for up to 10.5 billion people. An indirect way to help soil is to cut waste. Today, we throw away up to 40 percent of our food, which means we waste a huge amount of natural resources. Beyond landfills, food waste produces 8 percent of global methane emissions. San Francisco has set an example by making composting mandatory. In France, grocers who throw out food can face two years in jail. Another good case is the Imperfect Produce initiative, which collects ugly but edible food, like a carrot with two legs, and gives it a chance to reach customers who care. You can cut your own waste by starting a compost pile. Fewer fertilizers also mean less methane and nitrous oxide, and the latter is 300 times more harmful than carbon dioxide.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text: The Congressional Research Service predicted that the cost of health programs will make up almost half of mandatory federal spending in 2025, and that figure is alarming. To avoid this, preventing illness is essential, and healthy food is one of the most effective ways to do it. Experts say that even a 20 percent increase in fruit and vegetable consumption could make a difference, and if Medicare and Medicaid patients follow this advice, the rate of cardiovascular disease could drop by 1.93 million cases. Food is leverage, and by using it, we can tackle many challenges. Soil restoration will bring more nutritious harvests and reduce the greenhouse effect. Lobbying reform will make it easier to pass important laws and force companies to pay for the illnesses their products cause. Subsidy reform will encourage farmers to grow more vegetables and fruits. Bonuses for buying nutritious food with food stamps will let low-income families save on medical costs. A junk food ban will protect our kids mental and physical health. We can move toward these changes by buying healthy products, voting for decent candidates, and supporting nonprofits. Sometimes, sticking to tradition is the best move. In the 1960s, African American diets were full of nutrients, from good fats to fiber, and scientists believe that diet was twice as beneficial as an average one. Our awareness and commitment to a healthy diet will encourage others to follow this wholesome path. Try this. Look at your usual shopping list and choose alternatives to unhealthy products. Check the voting records of politicians and support those who protect your health, not corporate profits. Watch the documentary Broken Brain to discover how food shapes mental health. Read it till the end?
